result:
I keep a small notebook in the inside pocket of every coat I own. The notebooks are identical—black covers, unruled pages, stitched spines that crackle like wet kindling when you open them flat—yet each one ends up a different country. The coat I wear for rain hosts a damp archipelago of smeared ink where thoughts arrived too quickly for the paper to dry. The winter coat’s notebook is brittle with cold; the letters stand upright like frostbitten soldiers who refuse to desert their post. The lightweight jacket I pack for travel carries a notebook that smells faintly of every city I’ve passed through: cardamom, jet fuel, limestone dust, the metallic bite of foreign laundromats. I never set out to create these micro-territories; they annex themselves while I’m busy looking the other way.

Language behaves differently when it believes no audience is watching. In the notebooks, verbs sprout extra syllables the way frost thickens on a windowpane, and nouns shuffle off their dictionary definitions like party guests slipping into someone else’s coat. A “chair” becomes not the object but the moment just before sitting, the small surrender of weight when the day allows itself to be folded and put down. “River” stops being water and turns into the sound a mind makes when it finally gives up trying to hold itself apart from time. I visit these sentences the way you might look in on a pet left with a neighbor: guilty, curious, relieved to find they have continued to breathe without me.

There is, I realize, a conspiracy between motion and memory. The kilometers I log on trains, in airports, on night buses with windows that double as black mirrors—those kilometers are not spent traveling so much as dissolving. Each jolt of turbulence, each station announcement in an alphabet I can’t read, shakes loose a layer of the self I was when I boarded. What rattles to the floor of the aisle is a brittle crust of certainties: my name, my reasons, the list of grievances I rehearse in the shower. Underneath, the newer skin is embarrassingly tender, pink as a scar that hasn’t decided whether to forgive the knife. I walk city streets at 3 a.m. feeling peeled, convinced the stray cats can see straight through to the pulse in my neck.

Once, in Sarajevo, I followed a trail of brass plaques embedded in the sidewalk. They are called Sarajevo Roses—mortar scars filled with red resin to mark where people died during the siege. At first I tiptoed around them, as though stepping on one might reopen the wound. Then a boy on a bicycle zipped across the rose I was trying to avoid, tires squeaking over the polymer blossoms. I watched him disappear into a side street that smelled of bread and coal smoke, and understood something too large for the notebook I was carrying: commemoration is not a shrine you guard; it is a road you agree to keep traveling, even when your wheels make the same ugly sound the shells once made. I bent down, touched the warm resin, and felt no explosion—only the faint vibration of the city’s heart learning a new rhythm.

I used to think memory was a cupboard you could tidy: fold this, discard that, line the shelves with paper scented of lavender intention. Now I know it is more like a river that refuses to stay inside its banks. Every time I return to a place, the river has brought new silt, carried off someone’s porch. The café where I once cried over a letter that began “I’m sorry this arrives too late” is now a storefront selling neon sneakers. The bench where an old man taught me to say “thank you” in Ladino has been chopped up and fed to a municipal wood chipper. I stand on the pavement holding my useless map, feeling the embarrassment of a ghost who discovers the house has been renovated without consultation. Yet if I close my eyes, the old café reconstitutes itself: the greenish lamp shade, the crack in the cup that looked like a lightning bolt, the way the waitress hummed out of key. Which version is truer—the physical footprint now hawking overpriced shoes, or the hologram that pulses behind my eyelids? The notebooks refuse to adjudicate; they simply record both and let the contradiction stand like an untied shoelace.

People ask whether I feel safe moving through the world alone, a question that always arrives in the conditional tense, as if danger were a separate country you could choose not to visit. I tell them the truth: I am safest when in motion, when the passport in my pocket still holds the faint warmth of the official who stamped it. Motion is a kind of exorcism; it shakes loose the paralysis that grows on stationary skin like mold on forgotten fruit. The real risks are the interior ones—the dream in which I cannot remember the way home, the sudden certainty that I have already lived the best day of my life and failed to notice. Those are the assassins who travel on forged papers, who speak the language of my own pulse. Against them, no embassy offers protection.

Still, there are moments of such gratuitous beauty that I forgive the world its sharper edges. Dawn in the Negev, when the desert sky peels from indigo to a pink so tender it feels like looking at the inside of your own eyelid. A woman in Tokyo handing me a packet of tissues because I was sneezing on the subway, bowing so deeply her glasses almost slid off. My granddaughter’s voice on a crackling video call, explaining that she has named her teddy bear after me because “he also can’t sit still.” These moments do not redeem anything; they refuse that ledger-book morality. Instead, they arrive like unmarked gifts left on a doorstep, containing exactly what you didn’t know you needed. I collect them the way children collect seashells: not for utility, but because holding them to the ear produces a sound that drowns out the fear.

I have begun to suspect that the notebooks will outlast me. Someday a stranger will buy a second-hand coat and discover the bulge in the lining. Or a street sweeper will find pages wind-wedged against a cemetery gate. The thought used to horrify me—the private made public, the misspelled words, the embarrassing sincerity. Now it comforts me. Let the sentences loose into the world the way you release a paper boat on a pond: without expectation of destination, only the small thrill of watching it float longer than physics said it should. Maybe the stranger will read a line and feel less alone. Maybe the street sweeper will use the page to wrap a handful of figs. Either way, something I touched will keep touching, an endless relay of fingerprints.

Night is folding itself over the city now; the neon sign across the street flickers between Hebrew and English, a heartbeat unsure of its language. I close the notebook, feel the spine protest, slip it back into the pocket above my heart. Tomorrow I board a plane whose destination I will not name here, because naming calls things into being before their time. Instead, I will sit in a metal tube with strangers who are all, like me, practicing the art of temporary disappearance. We will share recycled air and the illusion that distance is something you can consume like miniature pretzels. When we land, the river inside me will have shifted course again, and I will walk new streets with the same old bones, carrying the same blank notebook that is no longer blank. If you find this, know that I was here, and that I was trying—imperfectly, inexhaustibly—to pay attention.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
